subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 13! In this unit you will grow from a good adder and subtracter into an efficient and confident one. You already know how to add and subtract smaller numbers. Now you will learn to handle larger numbers up to 10,000 and beyond, to add and subtract decimals and money, to work with fractions that share the same denominator, and to solve multi-step word problems that mix both operations together. Most importantly, you will learn to choose the smartest method for each calculation instead of always doing the same thing.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Add and subtract numbers with up to four or five digits using efficient column methods.
Choose between mental and written methods depending on the numbers involved.
Use mental strategies: number bonds, compensation, partitioning and near doubles.
Add and subtract decimals and amounts of money, lining up the decimal points.
Add and subtract fractions with the same denominator, including answers that are improper fractions, and convert them to mixed numbers.
Estimate answers by rounding, and check calculations using the inverse operation.
Solve one-step and multi-step word problems and explain your reasoning clearly.

concepts.title: Column Addition with Larger Numbers
concepts.explanation: Column addition is a written method where we line up the digits by their place value and add each column separately, starting from the right (the ones column) and moving left. Whenever a column adds up to ten or more, we carry the extra ten into the next column. This carrying is called regrouping, because ten ones are regrouped into one ten. Let us work through 367 + 285 step by step. Copy each stage onto paper as you read.
concepts.example: Step 1 – line up the columns. Write 367 above 285 so the ones (7 and 5), the tens (6 and 8) and the hundreds (3 and 2) sit in their own columns.

concepts.title: Column Subtraction and Regrouping
concepts.explanation: Column subtraction also lines up the digits by place value and works from the right. The difference is that sometimes the top digit is smaller than the bottom digit in a column. When that happens we regroup – we take one from the next column to the left and add ten to the current column. Many people call this borrowing, but it is really an exchange: one ten becomes ten ones, or one hundred becomes ten tens. Step 1 – line up the columns. Write 367 above 185.

concepts.title: Mental Calculation Strategies
concepts.explanation: Not every calculation needs columns. For friendly numbers, a mental method is quicker and just as accurate. Here are four powerful mental strategies. Learn to recognise which numbers suit each one. 1. Number bonds. Use pairs you know add to 10, 100 or 1,000. Because 37 + 63 = 100, you can add 37 + 63 in your head instantly. Bonds to 100 are especially useful for giving change: from 100 shillings after spending 63, you know 37 comes back.
concepts.example: 2. Compensation. Add or subtract too much, then adjust. To work out 398 + 25, it is easier to add 400 + 25 = 425 and then take away the extra 2 you added, giving 423. To do 2,345 − 1,999, subtract 2,000 to get 345, then…

concepts.title: Choosing an Efficient Method
concepts.explanation: An efficient method finds the correct answer with the least effort and the smallest chance of error. Choosing well is a real mathematical skill. Before you calculate, pause and ask yourself three questions: How big are the numbers? Small or friendly numbers often suit a mental method; large or awkward numbers suit columns.
concepts.example: Is there lots of regrouping? Many carries or borrows make mental work risky – columns are safer.

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Decimals and Money
concepts.explanation: Money and measurements often come with decimals. The wonderful news is that decimals add and subtract exactly like whole numbers, with one golden rule: line up the decimal points. When the points are aligned, the ones sit under ones, the tenths under tenths and the hundredths under hundredths, and everything works as before. Suppose Khadija buys mango for 12.50 shillings and camel milk for 8.75 shillings. To find the total, line up the points and add: 12.50 + 8.75. Ones and tens: think of it as 1250 + 875 in…
concepts.example: Subtraction of money works the same way. From a 50.00-shilling note, after spending 37.60, the change is 50.00 − 37.60. Line up the points, regroup across the zeros, and you get 12.40 shillings. A helpful tip when…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Fractions (Same Denominator)
concepts.explanation: When two fractions have the same denominator – the same bottom number – adding and subtracting them is simple. You add or subtract the numerators (the top numbers) and keep the denominator the same. The denominator tells you the size of each part, and that size does not change when you combine parts. For example, 3/8 + 2/8: the parts are all eighths, so 3 eighths plus 2 eighths is 5/8. Likewise 7/10 − 3/10 = 4/10, which simplifies to 2/5. A very common mistake is to add the denominators too – writing 3/8 + 2/8 =…
concepts.example: Sometimes an addition gives an answer where the numerator is bigger than the denominator. This is an improper fraction, and it is greater than one whole. For 5/8 + 6/8, add the numerators: 5 + 6 = 11, keep the…

methods.title: How to add with the column method
steps: Line up the digits by place value.
Add the ones. If the total is 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry the ten.
Add the tens (plus any carry), then the hundreds, and so on to the left.
Read the answer from the columns and check it against your estimate.

methods.title: How to subtract with regrouping
steps: Line up the digits by place value.
Start at the ones. If the top digit is smaller, regroup from the next column left.
Subtract each column in turn, moving left.
Check with the inverse: add your answer to the number you subtracted.

methods.title: How to add or subtract fractions (same denominator)
steps: Check the denominators are the same.
Add or subtract the numerators only.
Keep the denominator unchanged.
Simplify, and change any improper fraction into a mixed number.

methods.title: Adding money with decimals
methods.example: Yusuf buys sambusa for 6.75 shillings and banana for 3.50 shillings. What is the total?
steps: Solution: Line up the decimal points: 6.75 + 3.50.
Hundredths: 5 + 0 = 5.
Tenths: 7 + 5 = 12, write 2 carry 1.
Ones: 6 + 3 + 1 = 10, write 0 carry 1.

methods.title: Adding fractions to an improper answer
steps: Solution: Add the numerators, keep the denominator: 5 + 4 = 9, so the answer is 9/6.
This is improper, so convert: 9 ÷ 6 = 1 remainder 3, giving 1 3/6, which simplifies to 1 1/2.
Check the adding fractions to an improper answer result carefully.

methods.title: Subtracting fractions and simplifying
steps: Solution: Subtract the numerators, keep the denominator: 7 − 3 = 4, so 4/10.
Simplify by dividing top and bottom by 2: 2/5.
Check the subtracting fractions and simplifying result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Column addition with carrying
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 4 + 7 = 11, write 1 carry 1. Tens: 3 + 6 + 1 = 10, write 0 carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 5 + 1 = 13, write 13. The answer is 1,301. Estimate check: 700 + 600 = 1,300, which is very close, so 1,301 is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Column subtraction across a zero
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 4 − 7 is not possible, so regroup. There are no tens to take from, so first regroup a hundred into tens (9 hundreds become 8 hundreds and 10 tens), then regroup one of those tens into ones (10 tens become 9 tens and 14 ones). Now ones: 14 − 7 = 7. Tens: 9 − 5 = 4. Hundreds: 8 − 6 = 2. The answer is 247. Check with the inverse: 247 + 657 = 904. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Choosing a mental method
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 1,999 is very close to 2,000, so use compensation. Subtract 2,000: 2,345 − 2,000 = 345. You took away 1 too many, so add it back: 345 + 1 = 346. No columns needed – the friendly number made this quick.

workedExamples.title: Adding money with decimals
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf buys sambusa for 6.75 shillings and banana for 3.50 shillings. What is the total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Line up the decimal points: 6.75 + 3.50. Hundredths: 5 + 0 = 5. Tenths: 7 + 5 = 12, write 2 carry 1. Ones: 6 + 3 + 1 = 10, write 0 carry 1. Tens: 1. Bring the point down: 10.25 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Adding fractions to an improper answer
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the numerators, keep the denominator: 5 + 4 = 9, so the answer is 9/6. This is improper, so convert: 9 ÷ 6 = 1 remainder 3, giving 1 3/6, which simplifies to 1 1/2.

workedExamples.title: Subtracting fractions and simplifying
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the numerators, keep the denominator: 7 − 3 = 4, so 4/10. Simplify by dividing top and bottom by 2: 2/5.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A library had 2,345 books. It received a donation of 567 new books, then removed 234 damaged books. How many books does it have now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Step 1, add the donation: 2,345 + 567 = 2,912. Step 2, subtract the damaged books: 2,912 − 234 = 2,678 books. Notice the problem needed two operations, one after the other.

workedExamples.title: Finding a missing number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: To find a missing part in an addition, use the inverse operation – subtract the known part from the total: 605 − 258 = 347. Check: 347 + 258 = 605. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Subtract the fractions and simplify
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract the fractions and simplify: a) 7/8 − 3/8 b) 11/12 − 5/12 c) 8/9 − 5/9.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Money: work out the change from 100.00 shillings after spending 63.40 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Check each calculation with the inverse operation: a) 347 + 258 = 605 b) 625 − 189 = 436.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Mixed fraction operations – give improper answers as mixed numbers: a) 5/6 + 4/6 b) 5/12 + 9/12 c) 13/8 − 5/8.

activities.title: The Market Stall Game
activities.materials: paper price tags, small stones or bottle caps as shillings.
steps: Set up a pretend market with items priced in shillings and decimals, such as mango 12.50 and camel milk 8.75.
One person is the shopper and picks three items; the other is the trader.
Add the prices by lining up the decimal points to find the total.
Pay with a round amount (say 50 shillings) and work out the correct change. Swap roles and play again.
This trains you to add money accurately and to subtract for change – exactly what real traders do every day.

activities.title: Column Method Race
activities.materials: squared paper, a pencil, a timer.
steps: Write five four-digit addition questions and five four-digit subtraction questions.
Estimate each answer first by rounding to the nearest hundred.
Solve each one using the column method, lining up the digits carefully.
Check every answer with the inverse operation. Score one point for each correct and checked answer.

activities.title: Fraction Strips of Anjero
activities.materials: strips of paper (pretend they are anjero or a chapati).
steps: Fold and cut a strip into equal parts, for example eighths, and shade some parts.
Use two strips to model additions like 5/8 + 6/8 by combining shaded parts.
When you get more than one whole, tape a fresh strip on to show the improper fraction as a mixed number.
Model subtractions too, such as 7/8 − 3/8, by covering up parts.

activities.title: Estimate-Then-Count Investigation
activities.materials: a large pile of small objects (beans, dates, stones).
steps: Split the pile into two heaps and estimate how many are in each by rounding.
Add your two estimates to predict the total.
Now count each heap exactly and add the real amounts using a written method.
Compare your estimated total with the exact total. Repeat three times – do your estimates get closer?

activities.title: Missing-Number Detective (Family Game)
activities.materials: paper and pencil, one family member as the puzzle-setter.
steps: The setter writes a calculation with one number hidden, such as □ + 258 = 605.
The detective uses the inverse operation to find the missing number.
The setter checks by putting the number back into the calculation.
Swap roles and make the numbers larger each round to increase the challenge.

activities.title: Journey Distances Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a list of distances between East African cities (real or invented).
steps: Write down distances such as Mogadishu to Kismaayo, Nairobi to Mombasa, Hargeisa to Berbera.
Add two legs of a journey to find the total distance travelled.
Subtract to find how much farther one journey is than another (the difference).
Estimate each answer first, then calculate exactly and check with the inverse operation.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you most with lining up columns or decimal points? Which mental strategy do you now use without thinking? What was the trickiest part, and what would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Column Method Rule
rules.text: Line up ones under ones, tens under tens, hundreds under hundreds. Work from the right. Carry in addition; regroup (borrow) in subtraction.

rules.title: Same-Denominator Fraction Rule
rules.text: Add or subtract only the numerators. Keep the denominator the same. If the numerator becomes larger than the denominator, change to a mixed number.

rules.title: Decimals and Money Rule
rules.text: Line up the decimal points, then add or subtract as with whole numbers. Bring the decimal point straight down into the answer. Fill empty places with zeros.

rules.title: Check-It Rule
rules.text: Estimate before you calculate by rounding. Check after you calculate using the inverse operation: addition undoes subtraction, and subtraction undoes addition.

terms: An approximate answer found by rounding
The opposite operation, used to check
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
Numerator equal to or larger than denominator
A whole number and a proper fraction together
commonMistakes: Use squared paper; ones under ones
Work one column at a time, slowly
Add numerators only; keep the denominator
Line up points; bring the point straight down
Look first; use a mental method when it fits

realProblems.prompt: A theatre in Mombasa sold 234 tickets on Friday, 356 on Saturday and 289 on Sunday. How many tickets were sold over the three days?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer had 5,678 apples. After selling some at the Garissa market, he has 2,345 left. How many did he sell?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A shop had 345 red balloons, 278 blue and 189 green. After Eid they had sold 267 red, 198 blue and 156 green. How many balloons of each colour are left, and how many in total?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: For iftar, Sumaya buys bariis for 22.50 shillings, sambusa for 15.75 shillings and dates for 11.00 shillings. What is the total, and what change does she get from 60 shillings?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

questions.question: Calculate 734 + 567.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 4 + 7 = 11, write 1 carry 1. Tens: 3 + 6 + 1 = 10, write 0 carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 5 + 1 = 13, write 13. The answer is 1,301. Estimate check: 700 + 600 = 1,300, which is very close, so 1,301 is sensible.

questions.question: Calculate 904 − 657.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 4 − 7 is not possible, so regroup. There are no tens to take from, so first regroup a hundred into tens (9 hundreds become 8 hundreds and 10 tens), then regroup one of those tens into ones (10 tens become 9 tens and 14 ones). Now…

questions.question: Calculate 2,345 − 1,999 in your head.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
questions.explanation: 1,999 is very close to 2,000, so use compensation. Subtract 2,000: 2,345 − 2,000 = 345. You took away 1 too many, so add it back: 345 + 1 = 346. No columns needed – the friendly number made this quick.

questions.question: What does “Sum” mean?
options: An approximate answer found by rounding
questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Sum means The answer to an addition.

questions.question: What does “Difference” mean?
options: An approximate answer found by rounding
The opposite operation, used to check
questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Difference means The answer to a subtraction.

questions.question: What does “Regrouping” mean?
options: An approximate answer found by rounding
The opposite operation, used to check
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Regrouping means Exchanging between place values (carry / borrow).

Work it out step by step, then check each option.

choices: The answer to an addition
The answer to a subtraction
Exchanging between place values (carry / borrow)
An approximate answer found by rounding
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Sum means The answer to an addition.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
rounds.explanation: Ones: 4 + 7 = 11, write 1 carry 1. Tens: 3 + 6 + 1 = 10, write 0 carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 5 + 1 = 13, write 13. The answer is 1,301. Estimate check: 700 + 600 = 1,300, which is very close, so 1,301 is sensible.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
rounds.explanation: Ones: 4 − 7 is not possible, so regroup. There are no tens to take from, so first regroup a hundred into tens (9 hundreds become 8 hundreds and 10 tens), then regroup one of those tens into ones (10 tens become 9 tens and 14 ones). Now…

rounds.clue: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Regrouping means Exchanging between place values (carry / borrow).

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 13 methods.
rounds.explanation: 1,999 is very close to 2,000, so use compensation. Subtract 2,000: 2,345 − 2,000 = 345. You took away 1 too many, so add it back: 345 + 1 = 346. No columns needed – the friendly number made this quick.

selfAssessment: I can add and subtract numbers with up to four or five digits using efficient column methods.
I can choose between mental and written methods depending on the numbers involved.
I can use mental strategies: number bonds, compensation, partitioning and near doubles.
I can add and subtract decimals and amounts of money, lining up the decimal points.
I can add and subtract fractions with the same denominator, including answers that are improper fractions, and convert them to mixed numbers.
I can estimate answers by rounding, and check calculations using the inverse operation.
I can solve one-step and multi-step word problems and explain your reasoning clearly.
